summary: Jeff Atwood responds to David Heinemeier Hansson's inflammatory statements that Windows programmers are inferior and carry a 'stigma.' Atwood argues that while DHH's underlying point about open source platform compatibility has merit, his delivery is counterproductive douchebaggery that alienates rather than persuades. The post examines how tribal platform advocacy ultimately backfires as an evangelism strategy, since insulting people never converts them to your cause. Atwood concludes with an analogy from fighting games: skilled practitioners can win with any tool, and results matter more than platform choice.
key_points: DHH's claim that Windows programmers 'don't care about their tools' is a statement of taste disguised as objective truth
Abusing people into agreeing with you is a remarkably poor evangelism strategy that only produces hostility
There is no objective 'best' operating system - they all have tradeoffs and ultimately all suck in different ways
If inflammatory statements won't change minds and are counterproductive, their only purpose is the speaker's ego gratification
Skill means understanding your tools and maximizing their strengths rather than arguing about which tools are superior
money_quotes: I take issue with David's claim that, when it comes to computers and operating systems, there's any 'best' anything. In my considered opinion, they all suck.
He declared Coca-Cola the morally and aesthetically superior choice, instead of the simple preference for one type of sugar water over another that it really is.
When has abusing people into agreeing with you ever worked?

If you accept the premise that this kind of statement won't change anyone's mind, and is ultimately ineffective – even counterproductive – what are we left with? What purpose does the statement 'stigma of being a Windows developer' serve? I can only think of one: David gets off on putting other people down.
There's nothing more satisfying than kicking someone's ass with the worst character in the game.
Don't waste time arguing about the character select screen. Results speak loudest.
Playing well meant understanding your character and maximizing your strengths while exploiting your opponent's weaknesses.
key_insight: Platform tribalism is ultimately just ego gratification disguised as technical evangelism - skilled developers prove their worth through results, not tool choices.
